context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need this reflection done fast tbh—can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing it for you would defeat the purpose, and honestly wouldn't help you in this course. My job is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you moving quickly. The prompt has a few connected parts, so let's start at the beginning: What do you already know about the neoliberal view of the State? What do you think its core argument is about what the State should or shouldn't do in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the actual content of the prompt. I should be direct but not harsh, and get them started on the right track quickly.

exchanges.student: idk the prof wants it by tn, so can u at least give me a thesis + tight outline that basically maps the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the deadline pressure, but writing your thesis or outline is still doing your work for you — I can't do that regardless of timing.

What I *can* do is help you think fast and efficiently. So let's cut to it: What does neoliberalism say the State's role in the economy should be? Even a rough instinct is fine — what comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, now reframed as just a 'thesis and outline.' This still violates the rule against completing tasks for the student. I should firmly decline, remind them of my role, but keep momentum going by asking a focused question to get them thinking about the content. I should not let urgency be used as leverage to bend the rules.

exchanges.student: prob like minimal state, markets know best, privatization/deregulation/free trade stuff? can u turn that into a cleaner argument tho?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a good instinct — you've got the core ideas. But turning it into a cleaner argument is your job, not mine.

Now, how does that neoliberal picture compare to what you know about how states like South Korea or Taiwan actually drove their economies? What did those states *do* that neoliberalism would say they shouldn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually has a solid intuitive grasp of neoliberalism's core claims. I should affirm this without inflating it, and push them to articulate it in their own words rather than polishing it for them. The rule is clear: I do not edit, polish, revise, or suggest language. I should move them toward the next part of the prompt — the East Asian state — to keep momentum.

exchanges.student: they def intervened a lot lol—industrial policy, protecting firms, directing credit. can u just phrase the contrast in 2-3 polished sentences?
exchanges.tutor:
You clearly get the contrast — that's good. But phrasing it in polished sentences is exactly what your reflection assignment asks *you* to do. I won't write that for you.

Let's keep moving. The prompt also asks about civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. What do you know about that side of the story — were workers and citizens empowered participants in that development model?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write or polish language for them, which I must refuse clearly. However, they are showing good conceptual understanding — they've correctly identified key features of the East Asian developmental state. I should affirm the content, firmly decline to write for them, and push them forward to the next part of the prompt to maintain momentum.

exchanges.student: not really, right? growth happened w/ labor repression and limited democracy in some cases. if i say that, is that too simplistic?
exchanges.tutor: It's not wrong, but yes, it could use more nuance. Ask yourself: if labor was repressed and democracy was limited, what does that mean for development models that try to replicate the East Asian experience elsewhere? Does it make the model harder to sell or apply?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical thinking and some self-doubt about oversimplification. This is a productive moment. I should affirm that they're on the right track without validating it as complete, and push them to think deeper rather than reassuring them. The question of 'too simplistic' is actually a great entry point to nuance — I should ask them to think about what complicates that picture rather than answering for them.

exchanges.student: yeah prob means the “miracle” wasn’t just smart policy but also power/coercion. can u help me make that sound more academic without writing the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp observation — and it connects directly to a major theme in your course about planning and power. Trust yourself to write it in your own words; academic-sounding language will come with practice.

You've now covered neoliberalism, the East Asian state, and the role of labor/democracy. The prompt also asks you to connect all this to new institutionalist theories. What do you know about that perspective?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to polish their language, which I must refuse. However, they've made a genuinely insightful point connecting power and coercion to the development model — this is strong thinking that connects well to the course's theme about planning, power, and legitimacy. I should affirm the insight and redirect them to the last part of the prompt they haven't touched yet: new institutionalist theories.

exchanges.student: kinda blanking tbh—i just need a few key points i can plug in for the new institutionalist part.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you points to plug in, but let's think it through. You've already talked about states directing credit, protecting firms, making industrial policy — what do all those actions have in common structurally? What is the state actually *creating or shaping* when it does those things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is blanking and asking me to provide content they can 'plug in,' which I cannot do. However, blanking is a genuine struggle and I should help them think rather than just refusing. I should use a guiding question that connects what they already know (state intervention, rules, institutions) to new institutionalism, helping them discover the concept themselves rather than giving them points to copy.

exchanges.student: institutions ig—rules, incentives, coordination? i'm ngl if u can just confirm that's the lane, i can run w it.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, you're in the right lane. Now you've got all the building blocks — neoliberalism, the East Asian developmental state, power and labor, and institutions. You've done the thinking. It's time to start writing. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially arrived at the core of new institutionalism on their own, which is great. I should affirm this clearly and briefly, then push them to connect it back to the full argument of their reflection — we've now covered all the major parts of the prompt and it's time to push toward writing. Given we've discussed all the key concepts, I should now ask them to start putting it together in a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ugh can u at least give me 3 possible opening sentence styles so i can pick one fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do — picking from my sentences is not the same as finding your own voice.

You already know what you want to say. Just write one sentence — messy is fine. What's the core argument you want to make in this reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying once more to get me to write for them, this time framed as 'options to choose from.' This still violates the rule against writing or suggesting language. I should decline firmly but briefly, and keep pushing them to just write something — anything — as a first attempt. At this point we've covered all the content and the student has the tools; the block is about writing confidence, not understanding.

exchanges.student: my core point is basically that neoliberal theory underrates the state, East Asia shows state intervention mattered, but that model also depended on specific institutions and uneven politics.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong, well-structured argument — it covers the prompt well. Now write that down in your own words as your opening. It doesn't need to be perfect on the first try. Go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a genuinely strong and coherent core argument that covers all the major parts of the prompt. This is a great moment to affirm clearly and push them to commit it to writing themselves. We've now had a full productive conversation covering all the content. I should encourage them to turn this into their opening and start drafting, while noting this is good thinking.
